title: 2025/12/01 - SSC (Part 4)
displayTitle: 2025-12-01 - Social Services and Community Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of the New Zealand Lotteries Commission [Part 4]
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of the New Zealand Lotteries Commission
description: Monday 1 December 2025 | Social Services and Community Committee 2024/25 Annual review of the New Zealand Lotteries Commission Hearing of evidence: New Zealand Lotteries Commission Mark Todd, Board Chair Jason Delamore, Chief Executive Jeff Hayward, Chief Financial Officer Will Hine, Head of Corporate Communications
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of the New Zealand Lotteries Commission. New Zealand Lotteries Commission. Mark Todd, Board Chair

chunks.text: Well, good afternoon. Welcome to the Committee Selection Committee. Um, so thank you for talking to us this afternoon. What I'd ask is if you could introduce yourselves, uh, to the committee and those watching online, and, uh, then ask you to, uh, you know, make an opening, um, statement. And then from there we'll move to questions from Emmas. Great. Thank you. Lieutenant Coto car. Thank you Chair, and good afternoon everyone.

Uh, so I'm Jason Delamore. I'm the Chief executive of, uh, the Laies Commission, which I'll call Lotto New Zealand. Uh, and with me, I've got chair by board Mark Todd next to me, Jeff Haywood, our Chief Financial Officer, and Will Hein, our head of corporate comms. So, good afternoon to you all.

Uh, so thanks for the opportunity to appear before you. As part of Lottos annual Review, we take our accountability to New Zealand as a and Parliament Extreme extremely seriously. Although Lotto does not receive taxpayer money, we are a Crown entity with a statutory monopoly. And the revenue we generate through Lawrie Games is ultimately public money that supports literally thousands of community organizations.

We see this, this review as an opportunity to demonstrate how responsibly and transparently we have delivered on our mandate. So Lotto is a bit of history. Lotto was established back in 1987 under the Gambling Act, and we exist to provide safe games that allow New Zealanders to play and win while contributing money back to Kiwi communities.

Through the Lotto Grants board. In the past 38 years, we have returned more than $6.7 billion back to communities. The Lotto Grants board is an independent government entity, and, and Lotto has no input into where that funding should be allocated. We aim to provide these strong, sustainable community returns while continually strengthening the safety of our products to reduce risks around problem gambling and underage gambling.

Everything we do is shaped by balancing these two things. The last time Lotto appeared in person before the committee was two years ago. Since then, we have reported two record financial results. We returned $434 million to the lot of grant board in FY 24 and $395 million in the past financial year, benefiting thousands of organizations.

I'm proud that we've done so well, keeping reports of gambling harm stable under our target of one person per 10,000 customers. In fact, on a per capita basis, gambling harm reports to the Ministry of Health related to Lotto have actually halved since 2017. Under our strategy to 2029, we are aiming to return $412 million in annual profits to the lottery grants board.

To do that, we've set a pathway for us to boldly transform our customer experience. A major step forward in this regard happened in September with a successful delivery of our Edge project. This involved replacing our core and online gaming system and updating the physical hardware in more than 1100 stores across the country.

Uh, this project was completed on time and within Budget Edge, and the projects that will follow are very important. It's crucial that we remain able to provide a modern lottery products safely and responsibly, given the rapidly changing competitive environment that we're in. And this includes the forthcoming licensing of online casinos within New Zealand.

Part of our statutory role is to advise the government on lotteries, and we've actively, actively engaged in this capacity in the past 12 months, noting the direction of the government around fiscal discipline. We are proud to have strict cost controls in place and close the year, 10.2% below our budget. One of the things I'm most pleased about is our strong and engaged workforce, especially being able to say that that's having just delivered a project that requires significant and sustained effort from the whole team

across the organization. Lastly, we're also very proud to be ranked number five on Kattar's Corporate Reputation Index in 2025, and that reflects our strong brand trust and connection to New Zealanders, and that improved from 12th place two years ago. So you have our responses to the annual review, uh, questions as well as our annual report and corporate planning documents, and we're welcome.

Any questions you may have. Thank you very much. Yeah. First Scott, thank you. Um, I appreciate you support Kiwis to play and win, but possibly use encourage Kiwis to play and lose more regularly. Would that be accurate? Uh, in terms of the odds? Yes. Yes. Thank you.

Um, but we are quite proud that, uh, it's all, it's all about lots of Kiwis just contributing a little bit. So our average ticket sales around $20 to put it into context. So, um, your, um, annual report states that harm minimization is central to everything we do. How much was spent by Lotto New Zealand in 20 24, 25 on harm minimization and harm minimization clause? I Don't have a number off the top of my head, Jeff, but we can talk about some of the things we do, but yeah, That's sort of embedded. So everyone at Lottos trained in harm, you know,

so we all attend the training. We have a dedicated harm team, um, so we could separate that out for you, but there's lots of activities, uh, that we do. So mystery shopping that comes at a cost to make sure that retailers are updated. Um, adhering to the harm guidelines, but I can come back to you with exact numbers on the whole, But we have a dedicated harm team.

A lot of our advertising is all around harmonization activity. A lot of our collateral, uh, that goes out to stores as well. So it's a significant part of what we do. Okay. I'd certainly appreciate that because given that this is central to what your purpose is, um, I would've thought that this was something that you'd, you would've put in your, um, in your response to us or you'd, you'd have have ready for us.

Um, so I guess possibly you don't know this, but how much does the spend on harm minimization differ from a previous period, um, previous reporting periods? Uh, do you know, do or is it, is it just one of the things you do The spend or, yeah, so we keep an eye on the outcomes and as I was saying before, the amount of, um, harm presentations over time has dropped.

Um, so in terms of outcome, that's a, that's a good result. But as Jeff looks at the numbers, we'll we'll have a look at it over, over the periods. Yeah, and I think if I, if I take a board perspective for that question, that the context of oversight of, uh, strategic direction harm minimization is woven into that consideration. Some of the key areas that, that we've initiated over the years that I've seen on the board, such as reduction in the number of retail retail store offerings in low decile areas have been integral to that, um, approach.

So it's an every year when you are doing your business planning and budgeting, um, um, minimization is a check that is a genuine headline item to actually consider in that regard. And just one, so you've talked about, um, meeting the target of, um, one in 10,000 players harm minimization, but there's a, there's a, a data gap, almost 5% data gap.

So how, how confident are you that you've actually met that, that target? Because it's not, there's, there's some gaps in the data. There's a small gap from the Ministry of Health data, so we are working with them to try and look at how they bridge that gap, uh, moving forward. But it's definitely something worth that. We, And the, and the gap and the measure has been a consistent measure over a number of years.

So the small periods of data gap probably, uh, something we could say that don't cause us great angst in the context of the long-term performance over years. New question Mau. Um, thanks very much. Um, my question relates to cybersecurity. So, um, h how confident are you that the system is secure both externally and internally?

What kind of safeguards have you employed to ensure that integrity? Sure. That answer could take a very long time that we, I we've invested, so our digital channel is very important to us. Uh, and we've got a very mature cybersecurity framework that sits, uh, around that. And we've increased our, um, investment in that over time.

Uh, so there's all sorts of preemptive, um, controls in place and detecting security issues. We've also got contracts with, um, third party experts that help us, uh, in the, uh, if an event did happen as well. And we are constantly doing exercises around cybersecurity. Sure. So you, um, it's up to that. So, um, we, I understand there's 52% of the sales are digital now.

Uh, no, that's retail. 48% digital. 48% is digital. Yeah. Yeah. Um, which is quite a high percentage. So, so you're absolutely confident the whole system is secure and I'm just aware that there's some very smart cookies out there. Yeah. Um, figuratively and literally speaking. Yeah. And um, you know, the, the kind of, um, infiltration that could happen could be quite devastating. Mm-hmm.

Yeah. So we have sophisticated monitoring looking at the system and specialist people, uh, within staff and as I was saying, contracting with real specialist third parties, uh, as well. Okay, Thank You. Your Question, Lamar? Uh, thank you Mr. Chair. I've, it's possibly, uh, a couple of subsets that sort of related to the early primary.

Um, my question relates to and think for the report that I've read, uh, my question relates to your system, uh, where you've said, uh, 48% is picking up from the previous question digital. Um, what, what I would want to ask is with, uh, your work with Ministry of Health problem gamblers.

So what, uh, tools are in place in terms of picking up through the system problem gamblers and what steps are in place? 'cause that's not anywhere here. It's quite an over. Sure. So, and we've got a, um, a program, fulsome program work that looks at prevention, detection and intervention. Yeah. So as people come and people might self-report to us through the call center.

So we've got mechanisms through that system to, um, capture folks and put 'em in touch with experts, uh, there and people can come in through the website, uh, as well. And that's on top of the Ministry of Health Stats. And so with that, with a lot of operations, uh, with different businesses moving online, uh, and then you've quoted a figure, I think it was 48%.

Do you see in terms of the forecast of that, some of those things will go online, there'll be a bigger percentage in people, maybe more at risk, So more people going to the digital channel? Is that the question? Yeah. Just To picture some of your products. Yes. Because it's moving to an online world Yes. Which could be a younger demographic. Yes.

So what is in place in terms of the sort of forecast of your Operation? Yes. So we've got some sophisticated tools, uh, that we roll out in turn that look at changes in, um, digital behavior. So if someone change, and this is what you need to look at, is the, um, change in behavior is normally an indicator. So if someone plays more often or they deposit, um, money in smaller, faster amounts and things like that, the system can detect that.

That's the detection part. And then we've got intervention. So it will go through to someone to send out an email or a call to intervene. And what you typically see is very high rates of people changing their behavior positively after that happens. Such A quick, just quickly, um, so would that system that, uh, you are confident will pick up things like problem gamblers and is it sort of Related? Yeah, from a behavioral perspective, yeah.

So it's an AI tool that, that learns over time and so constantly be changing and updating as new, new people come onto the system. Okay. And sorry, last, last, is there a self-exclusion sort of program that Yes. Yes, there is. Yep. And sorry, just to answer your question too, we have our cybersecurity audited by th third parties as well.

That gives us a higher degree of confidence. Oh, fabulous. Yeah. Some, some, um, global systems that audit us on cybersecurity side. So Scott, Yeah, so it's really good to hear that you've sort of, you're monitoring, you're able to intervene at people. But, um, the Ministry of Health data I understand is people self-reporting or seeking out help for problem, problem gambling.

Are you doing any other more proactive work to look into problem gambling people in difficulty? Is there any other way you are doing it independent research? Yes. That's, that's looking at It, that's the prevention part of prevention, detection and intervention. So we, we do an awful lot at the beginning in terms of educating people, making, um, we have a thing called the Place Mark program of work.

So we put things out both into retail and digital to help people think about what does responsible gambling look like. Um, you know, spend a little, um, we put out the odds we have that translated into different languages. We work with specialty groups, um, with other Maori, Pacifica and Asian, um, communities as well to try and educate those groups.

Um, but I'm, I'm, I'm really interested in whether you are, you've got any way of checking to see if there's a bigger cohort of people who have problem gambling other than those who self-report, Uh, through that detection tool that I talked about before that would give that to you through the digital channel, and then feedback from the retailers, uh, for the retail channel.

Yeah, and also just to keep in mind two aspects too to that, which is we have very low spend limits and we don't make a platforms available through late night and hours of the morning when many people who would actually be problem gaming, people would actually be ga would actually be gaming. So we effectively got a very strong attitude of prevention in the first place.

So your question is to detection, we'd like to think of a significant degree was actually dealt with by prevention first Stop everyone. What's a harm team? What makes up your harm team? So we have a group of folks who, um, are within the business and they're constantly looking at, um, advertising that we put out there.

So they'll, they'll review that for harm messaging and Credential. These people are, they like the qualified credential and they, uh, and they carry no bias? No. Oh, Really? Wow. Okay. We are very impressed with our, um, um, team and they hold us to account all day every day.

So, but they, they are evaluating all the collateral that goes out the door, advertising game design. Uh, we have other sophisticated tools around the world, around the world as well that look at game design and give you a score of that game around risk. And all of ours are either lowest medium, we have nothing that's in the high risk category, um, because we're a responsible government owned entity. So it does not behoove us to get into the risky part of, So does the design, uh, understand the cultural context, uh, in so with Maori families, um,

but not only just, just whanau, you know, and you've named different, um, groups in your report, does the design know that, understand that. Um, and then when you said ai, I thought, hmm, see that's another thing. So yeah, just, just does your design, do you have the people that it takes to understand those components and come up with the right design that is actually about how minimization and it can apply that? Yes.

So, uh, short answer is yes, and we are always trying to improve in that particular area. So as I was saying, we do work with different Maori and Pacifica groups, uh, and have just started working with different, um, Asian, Asian family services as an example as well, to try and tap into those groups to get a better understanding. So we have stakeholder sessions, we go out into communities, um, listen to people with lived experiences.

So we try to be as informed as we, as we possibly can. New question, David. Um, yes, you mentioned government agency, you're on the asset side of our ledger. Um, I'm interested in your response, and I think it was question 13, where you're looking forward to strategically you're responding to, uh, move towards digital transactions and evolving competitive environment.

That's actually what I'm asking about, including forthcoming licensing of online casinos in New Zealand. So things are changing, things are moving, so, you know, one of our assets. What are you gonna do to ensure that you remain that? Uh, it's not for us to make the call, I'm gonna remain an asset or not. It's up to us to make sure we are competitive in a, in a new marketplace.

So, so our investments, I talked about that core gaming system. So that gives us a modern core gaming system that we can operate in. And then our next set of projects, uh, what we call Project Frontier is around our web and app front end. So really deep improvements on the customer experience side on digital. So I've got no doubt once we get through that program, we, we, we, we are a world class lottery provider now.

We'll be even more world class in the digital footprint after we implement those. Okay. So you would see that you would maintain market share? Yeah. So, so good question. When you look at impacts of online casinos, uh, and other jurisdictions around the world, new Zealand's actually one of the last to, to do this. So core lottery businesses tend to stay relatively stable.

Some lotteries and every lottery is different around the world, but some of those lotteries that have got involved in sports betting or their own version of online casinos, they're the ones that face, um, the biggest impact when online casinos were regulated. But we don't have that. And, and just to add to that, 85% of the business is based on the, on the lotto suite of product.

The rest of it, the part product will be the part of the business that does under pressure undoubted. So our instant games and keynote are the areas that would be in the more competitive challenge. Yeah, yeah. I got a supplementary on that actually. 'cause if you look at the year in review, I think you said before that, uh, the last two years have been record years. Uh, so that's despite the competitive environment where, you know, increasingly people online and they, uh, you know, they can buy products globally.

Uh, so notwithstanding you have had record year, which seemed to indicate that you're doing pretty well in a pretty competitive environment. Yeah, we're doing, we're doing pretty well, but a record year at Lotto is often dictated by the, um, the jackpot, um, run that we have. And we've had the fortune of the last couple years having some big must be ones and and large jackpots.

And again, we've got, um, a proposal in front of government now for enhancing of our Powerball game and our five year strategy is around getting the right technology in place and the right shape of the game so that when, when we get to about 2029, we want around 400 odd million, $415 million reliably to be delivered to government and not as dependent on the large jackpot swings that we've seen in the last couple of years. So, so what Do you see as the, um, competitive horizon?

What do, what do you see as the biggest risk factors? Um, uh, well the first one is making sure we get the legislative and regulatory settings right and preserving lottos, um, play area, particularly in the, in the core gaming. So that's really essential, uh, for us. And then investing in the Powerball changes that we've got for government now, we'll take care of implementing the right systems to be, to be there and then we, we think we match fit, um, move forward.

Um, any question? Yes. Great. Um, of the 395 million that was, uh, transferred to Tu Ground Sport, you might not, or you might know this. Do you know what um, uh, what the figure is or figures awarded to Kapa Maori organizations, Maori led projects, do you Know? No, unfortunately we don't get that information so that Lotto Grants board would've that Totally separate. Yeah.

Yeah. So

let me address that in a couple other way. Lemme talk about product and then I'll talk about brand if you, if you like. So product wise, part of the benefits of the new gaming system is we, um, have a thing called the content hub. So that allows us to select games that are already made from around the world, bring them into New Zealand, customize 'em as necessary and bring them to market very quickly. We didn't, we never had that capability with the old system.

So now we, we can tap into a global set of products, um, that we can bring to market to the earlier questions. They have all sorts of harm minimization criteria over them as well, um, before we would put them out there into market. So that's the, so that's why we've got confidence around the product side. On the brand side, the investments we're making in Frontier will give us much better customer experience that would be able to compete with the online cas 'cause they're professional, um, in this area.

And what we don't want to give, want to give up is our special place in the hearts of New Zealanders pe people love the lotto brand and what it does to communities and we look forward to continuing that allocation of funds back into the community. 'cause they won't be able to compete with that. And we'll be known as, we'll be known as a very safe player around the world.

But that's, that's part of our strength preserve that going forward. Put putting aside, um, the sort of COVID era and, and the growth you saw and probably the use of people using digital platform during that period, what, what have you been seeing since then terms of, you know, you've got 1,140 stores I think across the country. We, we, your products are sold and then you have the digital space.

Have you been seeing an incremental change? Yeah, so it's, um, it's a good question. Peaked, um, through COVID and then it dropped back and we sort of see two to 3% a year increase in the, in the digital uptake. Um, and that'll, that'll continue and get another boost when Project Frontier that I talked about comes, comes to market.

However, it's a very strong retail network out there. And when you talk to customers, um, they love going and buying the the yellow ticket. So we don't wanna give up. Yeah, we don't wanna give up that local connection that people have. And one of the areas that we were working with a lot of grant sport is how do we tell those stories better at the community level? 'cause all that funding, all that profit literally goes into local schools, sports field and things like that.
